replies.author: @WorldTravelDoc
replies.text: Romania’s charm goes far beyond the myths, with breathtaking scenery and history

replies.author: @laurenceegan6136
replies.text:
​@TruePlanetOffical 

1. The people: I've made friends among the locals in some of the places I've visited over the years, but this has only ever happened in smallish cities and towns, yet on my first visit to Bucharest, quite a few (mostly older) people approached me to either ask why I came, or tell me about the history of the buildings and churches I was photographing; something unusual for a city the size of Bucharest.

2. The weather: I come from a country which experiences high rainfall within a narrow band of average temperatures over the course of a year, and the distinct seasons offered by a country like Romania is very appealing to me.

3. The countryside: my country has a reputation for awe-inspiring bleak landscapes, yet these are to a great extent the product of deforestation and other man-made changes in land use over centuries, and although many disastrous infrastructure projects during the Ceauşescu era have had a negative effect on the ecology of Romania in recent times, the abandonment of these projects* and - in some cases - attempts at reversing the damage caused by these projects have to a great extent saved the countryside and Danube Delta (although the man-made desert in Oltenia is still a problem that hasn't been rectified).

4. The wildlife: much the same as the previous point in that, due in great part to the deforestation of my homeland (and concurrent attempts at removing certain predators from the land), we long ago lost bears, wolves, and large cats from our wild spaces, whereas all of the above still retain a presence in Romania, and whilst the bears can cause problems for hikers such as myself and those who live in Transylvania, I like the fact that these creatures still roam the forests over there, where they belong.

Someone I know who was born in Romania but now lives in North America remarked that some of the scenes depicted in photographs I took on my last visit to Romania were simply "magical", and that's what I was trying to capture about those places at certain times of the day at certain times of the year.

I've visited over forty countries, and there are maybe only another two I feel this way about besides Romania.

*anyone visiting Bucharest looking for an example of this kind of thing should visit Văcăreşti Park just outside of the city centre.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: @laurenceegan6136 Wow, you've been back that many times? That's honestly incredible. I was expecting someone to say they'd visited once or twice, not almost every year for a decade! Reading your comment makes me feel like Romania must have something really special about it. What is it that keeps drawing you back year after year?

replies.author: @laurenceegan6136
replies.text:
Many times: first on a day trip (believe it or not) in the winter of 2016, and decided on that cold, grey day, I had to visit in the summer.

I returned every year when I could afford and there were no pandemic restrictions in place: 2017, 2018, 2019, 2022, 2023, 2024 and just returned from my last visit.

Much of what I had read about it was negative - including from mainstream media in the West, and in Reader's Digest literature - but most of this was either outdated, or exaggerated.

The stray dog problem from the era of systematisation is to all intents and purposes gone, you won't be bitten by a bat, violent crime is non-existent in the experience of a tourist, and whilst you may experience begging at the main train station in the capital (and should be wary of taxi scams there), it's completely safe - even for a lone traveller.

The only gripes I've ever had is the frequent moodiness at shop counters, booking offices, and even the occasional tourist information bureau on one occasion many years ago, but there are historical/cultural reasons for this (small talk in these places isn't really a big thing), and the people are generally warm, friendly and generous.

It's a more community-oriented society than those in the West, and rampant individualism is still not really a common thing.

comments.author: @hashishkumar
comments.text:
1. ROMAN(S)- Refers to the ancient Roman Empire, also to those who live in ROME, the capital city of Italy.
2. ROMANIA(N) -Refer to citizens or people from the country of Romania in Eastern Europe.
3. ROMA (also called ROMANI people)- Also termed GYPSY, they are a distinct ethnic group found across several European countries, having migrated to Europe from South Asia.
